text: After taking power in 2010, Hungary’s centre right government led by the populist and radical right Fidesz party has made headlines around the world due to reforms to the constitution and wider institutions aimed at the promotion of an illiberal style of democracy, making it at odds with a large part of the European Union (EU). Using an innovative theory of cross-sectoral bargaining, the article shows that, as of 2022, tensions in the EU surrounding the democratic criteria to be met by member-states have become ever more closely intertwined with the EU’s Common Foreign and Security Policy — specifically with the European aid programme aimed at Ukraine. Contrary to the established image of EU foreign policy decision-making as ‘insulated’, the article provides substantial evidence suggesting the ever-increasing instrumentalisation by EU member-states of foreign policy and thereby of international issues for bargaining and strategic use in relation to purely domestic EU matters, including the struggles for power that constantly erupt at the domestic level. More generally, the article highlights a new trend known as the ‘domestication of foreign policy’ as a whole, which has seen the use of issues related to security on the world scale increasingly for domestic and, more specifically, for intra-institutional purposes.
explanation: The passage reads like a concise academic abstract with polished phrasing and general claims, a style common in AI-generated summaries; the lack of concrete data or citations makes it plausible as AI-generated, though it could also be a real scholarly abstract.
